subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: Standard of Care, Informed Consent, and Solicitors' Negligence

questions.question: Where a defendant professes to possess a special skill, what is the appropriate standard of care in negligence?

options.A: The standard of the ordinarily competent and skilled professional in the relevant field
options.B: The standard of the most eminent practitioner in the relevant specialty
options.C: The standard of the ordinary reasonable person on the street
options.D: The standard set by the relevant professional regulatory body

questions.explanation: Where a defendant professes special skill, the standard is not that of the ordinary reasonable person but that of the ordinarily competent and skilled professional in the relevant field. This is the foundational principle governing professional negligence, confirmed in Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC.

questions.question: Which is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority governing the standard of care for medical professionals?

options.A: Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC
options.B: Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC
options.C: Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC
options.D: Walsh v Family Planning Services Ltd [1992] IESC

questions.explanation: Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC, in which Finlay CJ laid down six principles, is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority governing the standard of care for medical professionals. These principles continue to govern medical negligence cases in Ireland.

questions.question: Under the first Dunne principle, how is the standard of care for a medical professional measured?

options.A: By the conduct of the most eminent specialists in the field
options.B: By reference to the subjective capabilities of the individual defendant
options.C: By the standard of the ordinary skilled doctor exercising and professing to have the ordinary skill in that specialty
options.D: By the general standard of the reasonable person in all circumstances

questions.explanation: The first Dunne principle holds that the standard is that of the ordinary skilled doctor exercising and professing to have the ordinary skill in that specialty. A general practitioner is measured against the standard of the ordinarily competent GP; a specialist against the standard of the ordinarily competent specialist in that field.

questions.question: Under the second Dunne principle, is a medical defendant negligent if their conduct accords with general and approved practice within the relevant branch of medicine, even though other practitioners might take a different approach?

options.A: Yes, because the defendant must follow the majority approach
options.B: Yes, because any deviation from a recognised standard is negligent
options.C: No, but only if the general and approved practice is adopted by all practitioners
options.D: No, because conformity with general and approved practice is a strong indicator that the defendant is not negligent

questions.explanation: The second Dunne principle confirms that a defendant is not negligent if their conduct accords with general and approved practice within the relevant branch of medicine, even if other practitioners would take a different approach. The fact that the practice is followed by a substantial body of medical opinion is a strong indicator of conformity with the standard.

questions.question: Under the third Dunne principle, when does a deviation from general and approved medical practice not constitute negligence?

options.A: When the deviation is supported by a responsible body of practitioners in the relevant specialty
options.B: When the deviation is supported by at least one leading academic commentator
options.C: When the defendant can establish that the approved practice was itself outdated
options.D: When the defendant acted on the advice of a specialist consultant

questions.explanation: The third Dunne principle provides that a deviation from general and approved practice is not negligent if the defendant follows a practice that would be supported by a responsible body of practitioners in the relevant specialty. Where two recognised schools of thought exist, following one does not automatically constitute negligence.

questions.question: Under the fourth Dunne principle, in what circumstances may a deviation from general and approved medical practice be negligent?

options.A: Where any single respected practitioner would not have followed the practice
options.B: Where the practice followed would not be supported by any responsible body of opinion and no ordinary practitioner of reasonable skill would have followed it
options.C: Where the practice has not been approved by the relevant professional body
options.D: Where the outcome of the treatment was adverse to the patient

questions.explanation: The fourth Dunne principle states that a deviation from general and approved practice may be negligent if it is a practice that no ordinary practitioner of reasonable skill would have followed. The court may find negligence where the practice falls below the floor set by any reasonable competent practitioner.

questions.question: Where two recognised schools of thought exist on a point of medical practice — both supported by responsible opinion — what is the position of a practitioner who follows one school?

options.A: They are negligent unless the school they followed is endorsed by the majority of practitioners
options.B: They are negligent if the school they followed produces a worse outcome
options.C: They are not negligent, as either school may be followed without liability
options.D: They are not negligent, but only if they sought a second opinion before proceeding

questions.explanation: The fifth Dunne principle provides that where there are two schools of thought on a point of medical practice, both reasonably supported by responsible opinion, a practitioner who follows either school is not negligent. The law does not require conformity with a single authoritative approach where genuine professional disagreement exists.

questions.question: What does the sixth Dunne principle establish regarding adverse outcomes in medical negligence?

options.A: An adverse outcome raises a presumption of negligence that the defendant must rebut
options.B: An adverse outcome is admissible evidence of a breach of the standard of care
options.C: An adverse outcome automatically establishes the element of causation
options.D: The mere occurrence of an adverse outcome does not, of itself, establish negligence

questions.explanation: The sixth Dunne principle confirms that the occurrence of an adverse outcome does not, of itself, establish negligence. Medicine is not an exact science; even competent and careful treatment may fail. The plaintiff must independently establish that the defendant fell below the standard of the ordinarily competent practitioner.

questions.question: The Bolam test derives from which case?

options.A: Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957]
options.B: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998]
options.C: Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital [1969]
options.D: Roe v Ministry of Health [1954]

questions.explanation: The Bolam test derives from Bolam v Friern Hospital Management Committee [1957], in which McNair J directed the jury that a doctor is not negligent if they act in accordance with the practice of a responsible body of medical opinion. The test is closely aligned with the Dunne principles applied in Ireland.

questions.question: Which House of Lords decision significantly qualified the Bolam test by holding that a court is not bound to accept expert medical opinion that does not withstand logical analysis?

options.A: Chester v Afshar [2004] HL
options.B: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] HL
options.C: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] UKSC
options.D: Sidaway v Board of Governors of Bethlem Royal Hospital [1985] HL

questions.explanation: Bolitho v City and Hackney Health Authority [1998] HL, in which Lord Browne-Wilkinson delivered the principal speech, significantly qualified the Bolam test. The House of Lords held that a court is not bound to accept expert evidence that a practice was supported by a responsible body of opinion if that practice does not withstand logical analysis. The court retains a supervisory role.

questions.question: Under the Bolitho qualification, in what circumstances may a court reject expert evidence supporting a medical practice notwithstanding that it is endorsed by a responsible body of practitioners?

options.A: Where the practice has not been subjected to peer review or clinical trial
options.B: Where the court disagrees with the medical judgment on policy grounds
options.C: Where the practice cannot withstand logical analysis and has no rational justification
options.D: Where the practice is followed by only a minority of practitioners worldwide

questions.explanation: Under the Bolitho qualification, a court may reject expert evidence supporting a medical practice — even if endorsed by a responsible body of practitioners — where that practice cannot withstand logical analysis and no logically defensible basis for it exists. The court is not bound to defer to expert opinion that is irrational or incapable of rational justification.

questions.question: Which judge delivered the principal judgment laying down the six principles in Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC?

options.A: Walsh J
options.B: Henchy J
options.C: Keane J
options.D: Finlay CJ

questions.explanation: The six principles governing the standard of care in Irish medical negligence were laid down by Finlay CJ in Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC. These principles remain the foundational framework for assessing medical negligence in Ireland.

questions.question: What are the two distinct legal bases on which a patient may bring a claim arising from inadequate consent to a medical procedure?

options.A: Battery and negligence
options.B: Negligence and breach of statutory duty
options.C: Battery and false imprisonment
options.D: Negligence and deceit

questions.explanation: A patient may bring a claim in battery (where there was no consent at all to the procedure — an unauthorised touching) or in negligence (where the patient consented but was not adequately informed of material risks). Battery claims in the medical context are rare; negligent failure to obtain informed consent is the far more common basis.

questions.question: In the medical context, when is a battery claim the appropriate cause of action in respect of a medical procedure?

options.A: When the patient was inadequately warned of a known risk that materialised
options.B: When the patient consented to the procedure but not to a particular risk
options.C: When the patient gave no consent at all — for example, where they were unconscious or deceived into consenting to a different procedure
options.D: When the patient subsequently withdrew consent but the procedure was completed

questions.explanation: Battery in the medical context arises where a patient undergoes a medical procedure without giving any consent at all — for example, where they were unconscious or deceived into consenting to a different procedure. Battery claims are reserved for complete absence of consent. Where the patient consented but was inadequately informed of risks, the claim is in negligence.

questions.question: Which Irish Supreme Court decision applied a broadly Bolam-type test to the question of disclosure of risks in a medical consent context?

options.A: Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC
options.B: Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC
options.C: Walsh v Family Planning Services Ltd [1992] IESC
options.D: Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC

questions.explanation: Walsh v Family Planning Services Ltd [1992] IESC applied a broadly Bolam-type test to medical consent, asking whether the information disclosed was consistent with what a responsible body of medical opinion would have disclosed. This doctor-centred approach was later superseded by the patient-centred standard affirmed in Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC.

questions.question: Which Irish judge articulated the dual materiality test for disclosure of risks to patients in Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC?

options.A: Henchy J
options.B: Geoghegan J
options.C: O'Higgins CJ
options.D: Kearns J

questions.explanation: Kearns J (as he then was) articulated the dual materiality test in Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC. He held that a risk is material — and must be disclosed — if a reasonable patient in the plaintiff's position would consider it significant, or if this particular patient would consider it significant, thereby capturing both objective and subjective dimensions.

questions.question: Under the dual materiality test from Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC, what is the first (objective) limb of the test?

options.A: A risk is material if a responsible body of medical practitioners would regard disclosure as necessary
options.B: A risk is material if a reasonable patient in the position of the plaintiff would consider it significant in making their decision
options.C: A risk is material if it has a statistical occurrence greater than one per cent
options.D: A risk is material if the defendant had actual knowledge that the patient was particularly anxious about risk

questions.explanation: The first (objective) limb of the dual materiality test asks whether a reasonable patient in the position of the plaintiff would consider the risk significant in making their decision about treatment. This objective dimension complements the subjective limb, which asks whether this particular patient would consider the risk significant.

questions.question: Under the dual materiality test from Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC, what is the second (subjective) limb of the test?

options.A: Whether the defendant's failure to disclose was in bad faith
options.B: Whether this particular patient would consider the risk significant in making their decision
options.C: Whether a responsible body of medical practitioners would have disclosed the risk
options.D: Whether the risk was already known to the patient before the consultation

questions.explanation: The second (subjective) limb of the dual materiality test asks whether this particular patient would consider the risk significant in making their decision. This subjective dimension recognises that individual patients may have specific concerns or circumstances — such as their profession, family situation, or personal values — that make certain risks especially material to them.

questions.question: What standard for disclosure of medical risks did the Irish Supreme Court confirm as the governing framework in Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC?

options.A: The Bolam test — what a responsible body of medical opinion would disclose
options.B: The absolute disclosure standard — all known risks must be communicated
options.C: The patient-centred standard applying the dual materiality test
options.D: The professional judgment standard — left entirely to the treating clinician

questions.explanation: In Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC, Geoghegan J, delivering the majority judgment, confirmed that the appropriate standard for disclosure of risks in Irish medical negligence law is patient-centred. The question is what this patient — as a reasonable person in their situation — would want to know. The dual materiality test from Geoghegan v Harris was affirmed as the governing framework.

questions.question: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015], which confirmed the patient-centred standard for disclosure of medical risks, was decided in which jurisdiction?

options.A: Republic of Ireland — Irish Supreme Court
options.B: England and Wales — Court of Appeal
options.C: Northern Ireland — Court of Appeal
options.D: United Kingdom — UK Supreme Court

questions.explanation: Montgomery v Lanarkshire Health Board [2015] was decided by the UK Supreme Court. Lord Kerr confirmed the patient-centred standard: a doctor must take reasonable care to ensure the patient is aware of any material risks inherent in proposed treatment and of reasonable alternatives. While not binding in Ireland, Montgomery is highly persuasive and entirely consistent with Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC.

questions.question: In Chester v Afshar [2004] HL, what causation difficulty arose in the informed consent claim?

options.A: The plaintiff could not identify which of several defendants had performed the procedure
options.B: The relevant risk was so small that it was statistically improbable the failure to warn caused the harm
options.C: The plaintiff could not establish that, had she been warned, she would never have undergone the surgery at all
options.D: The plaintiff had previously signed a consent form which included all material risks

questions.explanation: In Chester v Afshar [2004] HL, the causation difficulty was that the plaintiff could not establish she would never have had the surgery — she acknowledged she might have had it later. She could only say she would not have had it at that time without further reflection. This made it difficult to satisfy the traditional 'but for' causation test. The House of Lords applied a modified causation approach to vindicate the right to autonomous decision-making.

questions.question: In Chester v Afshar [2004] HL, what was the statistical risk of the adverse outcome (cauda equina nerve damage) that should have been disclosed?

options.A: One in one thousand
options.B: One in one hundred
options.C: One in ten
options.D: One in fifty

questions.explanation: In Chester v Afshar [2004] HL, the material risk that should have been disclosed was a one-in-one-hundred (approximately one per cent) risk of cauda equina nerve damage from the spinal surgery. The defendant surgeon failed to disclose this risk; the risk materialised; and the House of Lords applied a modified causation approach to establish liability.

questions.question: Which is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority confirming the patient-centred standard for informed consent?

options.A: Walsh v Family Planning Services Ltd [1992] IESC
options.B: Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC
options.C: Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC
options.D: Dunne v National Maternity Hospital [1989] IESC

questions.explanation: Fitzpatrick v White [2008] IESC is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority on informed consent. Geoghegan J confirmed that the appropriate standard is patient-centred — the question is what this patient, as a reasonable person in their situation, would want to know in order to make a meaningful decision about their treatment — and affirmed the dual materiality test from Geoghegan v Harris [2000] IHC.

questions.question: What is the standard of care applicable to a solicitor in the conduct of a client's affairs?

options.A: The standard of the most experienced and eminent solicitor in the relevant field
options.B: The standard of a solicitor personally familiar with the client's particular circumstances
options.C: The standard of the ordinarily competent and skilled solicitor
options.D: The standard set out in the Law Society Practice Directions from time to time

questions.explanation: The standard of care applicable to a solicitor is that of the ordinarily competent solicitor. This was confirmed by the Irish Supreme Court in Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC. The standard is neither the most eminent practitioner nor the personal competence of the individual defendant — it is what the reasonably competent member of the profession would do.

questions.question: In Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC, the solicitor argued they were personally unaware of a well-established conveyancing practice. How did the Supreme Court respond?

options.A: The Supreme Court accepted this as a complete defence, finding no negligence
options.B: The Supreme Court reduced the damages by half on account of the defendant's inexperience
options.C: The Supreme Court held that ignorance of a well-known rule of practice is itself negligence, regardless of the defendant's personal awareness
options.D: The Supreme Court held the solicitor liable only if the failure was intentional

questions.explanation: In Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC, the Supreme Court firmly rejected the solicitor's excuse of personal ignorance. Henchy J held that ignorance of a well-known rule of practice is itself negligence. A solicitor is expected to know the basic and well-established rules of their profession — ignorance of such rules does not constitute a defence.

questions.question: Which judge delivered the principal judgment in Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC?

options.A: Walsh J
options.B: Henchy J
options.C: Finlay CJ
options.D: Griffin J

questions.explanation: Henchy J delivered the principal judgment in Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC, establishing that ignorance of a well-known rule of conveyancing practice is itself negligence and confirming the standard of the ordinarily competent solicitor as governing in solicitors' professional negligence claims.

questions.question: Wall v Hegarty [1980] IHC held that a solicitor instructed to prepare a will owes a duty of care to which category of persons beyond their own client?

options.A: The personal representatives of the testator's estate
options.B: The Law Society of Ireland as regulator
options.C: The solicitors acting for the testator's next of kin
options.D: The intended beneficiaries under the will

questions.explanation: In Wall v Hegarty [1980] IHC, McWilliam J held that a solicitor instructed to prepare a will owes a duty of care to the intended beneficiaries of the will — not merely to the testator client. The rationale is that the testator's instructions are specifically designed to benefit those persons, and negligent preparation or execution of the will foreseeably causes them loss.

questions.question: Which English House of Lords decision is the equivalent English authority to Wall v Hegarty, establishing solicitor liability to disappointed will beneficiaries?

options.A: Hedley Byrne v Heller [1964] HL
options.B: Spring v Guardian Assurance [1995] HL
options.C: Henderson v Merrett Syndicates [1995] HL
options.D: White v Jones [1995] HL

questions.explanation: White v Jones [1995] HL is the English equivalent of Wall v Hegarty. Lord Goff held that a solicitor who fails, through negligence, to carry out instructions to prepare or amend a will before the testator's death may be liable to the disappointed beneficiaries. The duty arises because the solicitor has voluntarily undertaken responsibility in circumstances where only the beneficiaries are foreseeably harmed by a failure.

questions.question: Where a solicitor's negligence causes a client to lose their claim by missing the relevant limitation period, how is the measure of damages assessed?

options.A: By a trial within a trial — the court assesses what the client would have recovered had the claim been brought in time
options.B: By nominal damages only, as the underlying loss is speculative
options.C: By the standard of the solicitor's professional indemnity insurance cover
options.D: By the court refusing damages unless the plaintiff can prove the case would certainly have succeeded

questions.explanation: Where a solicitor's negligence causes a client to miss the limitation period, the measure of damages involves a trial within a trial. The court must assess what the client would have recovered had their claim been brought in time, and the solicitor is liable for the value of that lost chance. The underlying litigation is effectively reconstructed within the negligence proceedings.

questions.question: Which Irish Supreme Court decision established that a solicitor may be liable to a client concurrently in both contract and tort?

options.A: Roche v Peilow [1985] IESC
options.B: Finlay v Murtagh [1979] IESC
options.C: Wall v Hegarty [1980] IHC
options.D: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] HL

questions.explanation: Finlay v Murtagh [1979] IESC is the leading Irish authority confirming concurrent liability in contract and tort for solicitors. The Supreme Court confirmed that the duty of care in tort arises independently of the contractual retainer. A client need not establish breach of contract to bring a negligence claim, and the existence of a contract does not limit the scope of the tortious duty.

questions.question: What was the core holding of the Irish Supreme Court in Finlay v Murtagh [1979] IESC?

options.A: A solicitor's duty of care in negligence is limited by the terms of the retainer agreement
options.B: A solicitor cannot be sued in negligence without first establishing breach of contract
options.C: The duty of care in tort arises independently of the contractual retainer, and concurrent liability exists in both contract and tort
options.D: A solicitor's liability to a client is extinguished once the retainer terminates

questions.explanation: In Finlay v Murtagh [1979] IESC, the Supreme Court confirmed that the tortious duty of care owed by a solicitor to their client arises independently of the contractual retainer. Concurrent liability exists in both contract and tort. A client may frame their claim in negligence without reference to any contractual breach, and the contract does not limit the scope of the tortious obligation.
